title: Extended VC-dimension and Radon Type Theorems for Unions of Convex Sets
speaker: Noga Alon
abstract:
Simonyi Hall 101 and Remote Access
We define and study an extension of the notion of the VC-dimension of a hypergraph and apply it to establish a Tverberg type theorem for unions of convex sets. We also prove a new Radon type theorem for unions of convex sets, settling an open problem posed by Kalai in the 1970s.
Joint work with Shakhar Smorodinsky.

proof_stages.label: Classical convexity, Radon, and Tverberg setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes Euclidean convexity notation, recalls Radon's theorem and Tverberg's theorem, and reformulates them as statements about partitions whose containing convex sets must intersect.

proof_stages.label: Kalai's s-convex Radon problem and target bound
proof_stages.summary: An s-convex set is defined as a union of s convex sets, the Radon-type number f(d,s) is introduced, prior Ramsey-type finiteness is recalled, and the goal is stated as an upper bound of order d s^2 up to logarithmic factors, with analogous off-diagonal variants.

proof_stages.label: VC-dimension dictionary for Radon partitions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines shattering and VC dimension, observes that Radon partitions correspond to non-shattering by halfspaces, and recalls the Sauer-Shelah lemma controlling projections of finite-VC set systems.

proof_stages.label: Reduction of the s-convex Radon bound to two lemmas
proof_stages.summary: For the hypergraph whose edges are unions of s polytopes with at most s facets each, two lemmas are stated—geometric separation and a Boolean-operation VC bound—and these imply f(d,s) is at most the resulting VC dimension plus one.

proof_stages.label: Proof of separation and Boolean VC lemmas
proof_stages.summary: The separation lemma is proved by separating each convex component from each opposing component by halfspaces, and the VC lemma is proved by applying Sauer-Shelah to the number of possible traces under Boolean combinations, with discussion of why arbitrary Boolean operations on convex sets do not automatically preserve the geometric separation step.

proof_stages.label: Tverberg-type s-convex problem and r-VC dimension
proof_stages.summary: The Tverberg analogue f_r(d,s) is formulated for r parts and r s-convex supersets, the announced bound is given, and r-shattering/r-VC dimension is defined so that the case r=2 recovers ordinary VC dimension.

proof_stages.label: r-VC and geometric lemmas imply the Tverberg bound
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states that ordinary VC dimension d bounds r-VC dimension by O(d r^2 log r), states the corresponding r-fold geometric separation lemma with s^{r-1} facets, sketches how these yield the Tverberg-type theorem, and proves the r-VC bound by counting r-tuples of traces and partitions.

proof_stages.label: Abstract convexity extensions and exact-bound questions
proof_stages.summary: Because the proofs are largely combinatorial, they extend to separable abstract convexity spaces, yielding bounds relating Radon-type and Tverberg-type numbers, and the talk closes with questions about sharp constants and planar lower-bound configurations.

examples.name: Four planar points for Radon's theorem
examples.note: The convex quadrilateral case and the triangle-with-interior-point case show visually how four points in the plane split into two parts with intersecting convex hulls.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker discusses the two four-point planar configurations and indicates the corresponding Radon partitions whose convex hulls intersect

examples.name: Concave-arc planar configuration for s-convex lower bounds
examples.note: A polygon whose sides are replaced by concave arcs carrying many points is proposed as a planar configuration that resists the desired two-part s-convex Radon partition and may indicate the exact planar behavior.
examples.engagement_reason: the concave-arc construction is built and then used to show how any red-blue partition can be covered by disjoint s-convex sets via convex hulls chosen along the arcs
